questions.q: To which dynasty did the emperor Ashoka belong?
questions.explanation: Ashoka was the third ruler of the Mauryan dynasty, which had been founded by his grandfather Chandragupta Maurya.

questions.q: In which century BCE did Ashoka rule the Mauryan Empire?
questions.explanation: Ashoka reigned in the 3rd century BCE, conventionally dated from around 268 BCE to 232 BCE.

questions.q: Which religion did Ashoka embrace after the Kalinga war?
questions.explanation: Remorseful at the slaughter of the Kalinga war, Ashoka turned toward Buddhism and a policy of moral conduct.

questions.q: Ashoka was the grandson of which Mauryan founder?
questions.explanation: Ashoka was the grandson of Chandragupta Maurya, the founder of the Mauryan Empire.

questions.q: What was the capital city of Ashoka's Mauryan Empire?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan Empire under Ashoka was governed from Pataliputra, near modern Patna in Bihar.

questions.q: On what materials did Ashoka inscribe his famous edicts?
questions.explanation: Ashoka spread his moral edicts by inscribing them on rocks and polished stone pillars across the subcontinent.

questions.q: Which famous conquest is said to have triggered Ashoka's change of heart?
questions.explanation: The bloody conquest of Kalinga filled Ashoka with remorse and led him to renounce war.

questions.q: Who was Ashoka's father and the Mauryan emperor before him?
questions.explanation: Ashoka succeeded his father Bindusara, the second Mauryan emperor.

questions.q: What is the name of the moral policy Ashoka promoted in his edicts?
questions.explanation: Ashoka promoted dhamma, a policy of ethical conduct, tolerance, and non-violence, recorded in his edicts.

questions.q: Ashoka sent missions to spread his religion to which neighbouring island?
questions.explanation: Ashoka famously sent Buddhist missions abroad, including one to Sri Lanka, traditionally led by his son Mahinda.

questions.q: By what regnal title or epithet is Ashoka most often referred to in his own edicts?
questions.explanation: In his inscriptions Ashoka is called Devanampiya Piyadasi, meaning beloved of the gods, of gracious countenance.

questions.q: In which present-day Indian state was the kingdom of Kalinga, conquered by Ashoka, located?
questions.explanation: Kalinga corresponded broadly to the coastal region of present-day Odisha.

questions.q: Approximately how long after his coronation did the Kalinga war take place, according to his edicts?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's edicts record that the Kalinga conquest occurred about eight years after his consecration as king.

questions.q: Officials appointed by Ashoka to spread and oversee dhamma were known as what?
questions.explanation: Ashoka created a class of officers called Dhamma Mahamatras to promote and supervise his moral policy.

questions.q: In which script are most of Ashoka's edicts in India written?
questions.explanation: Most of Ashoka's edicts within India are written in the Brahmi script, though some northwestern ones use Kharoshthi and Greek.

questions.q: Which Greek-influenced languages appear in Ashoka's edicts found in the far northwest of his empire?
questions.explanation: Edicts from Kandahar and the northwest were inscribed in Greek and Aramaic for local populations.

questions.q: Which third-century-BCE Buddhist council is traditionally associated with Ashoka's patronage?
options: The Third Buddhist Council at Pataliputra
The First Buddhist Council at Rajagriha
The Fourth Buddhist Council in Kashmir
The Second Buddhist Council at Vaishali
questions.explanation: Tradition holds that the Third Buddhist Council was held at Pataliputra under Ashoka's patronage, presided over by Moggaliputta Tissa.

questions.q: Which emblem from an Ashokan pillar was later adopted as the State Emblem of India?
questions.explanation: The Lion Capital that crowned Ashoka's pillar at Sarnath became the State Emblem of independent India.

questions.q: The Sri Lankan chronicles that recount Ashoka's life and missions include which famous text?
questions.explanation: The Sri Lankan chronicle Mahavamsa, along with the Dipavamsa, preserves accounts of Ashoka and his Buddhist missions.

questions.q: Which welfare measure is frequently mentioned in Ashoka's edicts?
options: Building a navy for overseas trade
Planting trees and digging wells along roads
questions.explanation: Ashoka's edicts describe planting shade trees, digging wells, and providing medical care for people and animals.

questions.q: Roughly how many years is Ashoka traditionally held to have reigned?
questions.explanation: Ashoka is generally said to have ruled for around 37 years, from about 268 to 232 BCE.

questions.q: Which scholar's reading of inscriptions in 1837 helped identify the king 'Piyadasi' of the edicts with Ashoka?
questions.explanation: James Prinsep deciphered the Brahmi script in the 1830s, enabling the king 'Devanampiya Piyadasi' to be identified with Ashoka.

questions.q: Two separate rock edicts, distinct from the main series, were addressed specifically to the people of which conquered region?
options: Kalinga (the Separate Rock Edicts at Dhauli and Jaugada)
questions.explanation: The two Separate Rock Edicts, found at Dhauli and Jaugada in Odisha, were addressed to the officials and people of Kalinga.

questions.q: The legendary account of Ashoka's life and his patronage of Buddhism is preserved in which Sanskrit text?
questions.explanation: The Ashokavadana, part of the Divyavadana, is the main Sanskrit legendary biography of Ashoka.

questions.q: Several contemporary Hellenistic kings are named as recipients of Ashoka's dhamma missions in which of his edicts?
options: The Minor Rock Edict at Maski
questions.explanation: Rock Edict XIII names contemporary Hellenistic rulers (a king Antiyoka usually identified with Antiochus, along with Ptolemy, Antigonus, Magas, and Alexander) among the places his dhamma missions reached.

questions.q: Which inscription found at Maski was significant because it explicitly names the king as 'Ashoka'?
questions.explanation: The Maski Minor Rock Edict was important because it used the personal name 'Ashoka' rather than only the title Devanampiya Piyadasi.

questions.q: Ashoka's pillar at Lumbini commemorates which event with a tax remission for the village?
options: The first sermon of the Buddha
questions.explanation: The Rummindei (Lumbini) pillar inscription marks Ashoka's visit to the Buddha's birthplace and records a reduction of the village's tax burden.

questions.q: Which queen of Ashoka is named in a minor pillar inscription at Allahabad regarding her religious gifts?
questions.explanation: The so-called Queen's Edict on the Allahabad pillar records the charitable donations of Ashoka's queen Karuvaki.

questions.q: According to Buddhist tradition, in which Mauryan city did Ashoka serve as a viceroy before becoming emperor?
questions.explanation: Buddhist legends describe Ashoka governing as a Mauryan viceroy at both Ujjain and Taxila before his accession.

questions.q: Which short-lived dynasty supplanted the Mauryas within about fifty years of Ashoka's death?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan Empire declined after Ashoka and was overthrown around 185 BCE by Pushyamitra Shunga, founder of the Shunga dynasty.
